case_name: Balancing Glycol bromide and radium control from brackish groundwater for compact potable reuse polishing
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for brackish groundwater containing 1,2-Dibromoethane and radium intended for potable reuse polishing under strict effluent limits, limited footprint, and moderate cost constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: An inland water reuse facility plans to blend highly treated municipal reuse water with a compact sidestream from local brackish groundwater, but the groundwater contains Glycol bromide and elevated radium. The groundwater also has high TDS and hardness, creating a trade-off between broad removal performance and the burden of concentrate or residuals handling. The utility has limited building footprint and can support moderate operator attention, but it wants to avoid a treatment train that is excessively expensive or difficult to keep in compliance at very low finished-water targets.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should reliably achieve very low Glycol bromide concentration and combined radium control suitable for potable reuse blending, with stable compliance under variable groundwater salinity.
secondary_goals: minimize footprint expansion at the reuse site
limit concentrate or radioactive residual handling difficulty
control hardness and scaling only to the extent needed for reliable operation

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: radioactive residuals and brine disposal should be minimized because on-site handling options are limited
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: treatment should remain reliable despite seasonal TDS and hardness fluctuations and should avoid frequent performance instability near strict effluent limits

core_unit_functions.pre_oxidation_and_aeration: strip some volatile organics and condition water for downstream adsorption
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_lead_lag: adsorb Glycol bromide with staged beds to reduce breakthrough risk
core_unit_functions.strong_acid_cation_softening_for_radium: remove radium by ion exchange using hardness/radium selectivity mechanisms
core_unit_functions.cartridge_filtration: protect polishing units and capture fines
core_unit_functions.uv_advanced_oxidation_polishing: provide final polishing barrier for trace organics and improve confidence at strict effluent limits
core_unit_functions.final_stabilization_and_disinfection: restore finished-water stability and provide potable disinfection residual

applicability_rationale: This train separates the two contaminant problems into unit processes well matched to each mechanism: adsorption/polishing for Glycol bromide and cation exchange for radium.
Lead-lag GAC is a practical compact approach for low-ug/L halogenated organics and provides operational buffering against breakthrough under strict finished-water targets.
Radium removal by strong acid cation exchange is well established for groundwater and can fit a relatively small footprint compared with large clarification systems.
Adding UV-AOP as a polishing step improves confidence for potable reuse applications where the organic effluent target is stringent, without making membranes the primary treatment barrier.
Compared with full RO, this route avoids continuous high-volume brine generation while still offering strong compliance assurance.
potential_risks: Spent GAC replacement scheduling is critical because Glycol bromide breakthrough may be difficult to detect without targeted monitoring.
Softening resin regeneration waste can contain radium and hardness salts and must be managed as regulated residuals.
UV-AOP adds energy and requires lamp maintenance; it is polishing insurance rather than the primary removal mechanism.
Pre-aeration effectiveness for Glycol bromide is limited and should not be relied on alone.

core_unit_functions.air_stripping_packed_tower: remove volatile Glycol bromide from water phase
core_unit_functions.offgas_vapor_phase_gac: capture stripped volatile contaminants from the air stream
core_unit_functions.strong_acid_cation_softening_for_radium: remove dissolved radium through cation exchange softening

applicability_rationale: Glycol bromide is a volatile halogenated organic, so packed-tower air stripping is technically strong and often cost-effective for the contaminant itself.
The addition of vapor-phase GAC on the off-gas side addresses air emissions and is consistent with public guidance for VOC stripping systems.
Cation exchange remains a compact and proven radium control step downstream or in parallel.
This route can have lower liquid-phase media consumption than GAC-only treatment for the organic contaminant.
potential_risks: Tower height and ancillary off-gas equipment can be difficult under severe indoor footprint or building envelope constraints.
Air stripping efficiency can be affected by temperature and hydraulic loading, requiring careful design verification.
Off-gas GAC changeout and radium-bearing regeneration waste create two separate residual streams to manage.
This route provides less of a final dissolved-organic polishing buffer than lead-lag liquid-phase GAC plus UV-AOP.

core_unit_functions.chemical_softening_clarification: precipitate hardness and co-remove radium with solids separation
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb residual Glycol bromide and other trace organics
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove carryover solids and polish clarified water

applicability_rationale: Lime or lime-soda softening is a recognized method for radium reduction in hard groundwater and can simultaneously reduce scaling tendency.
Follow-on GAC provides a practical barrier for the trace organic contaminant.
This route can be attractive when hardness reduction is also desired for blending or downstream stability.
The process is robust for radium but is more infrastructure-intensive than ion exchange.
potential_risks: Large footprint for reactors, clarifiers, sludge handling, and filters conflicts with the site's space limitation.
Chemical sludge may contain concentrated radium and requires careful residuals management.
Operator attention for pH control, sludge handling, and process stability is higher than for compact adsorption and ion exchange systems.
GAC alone still requires tight monitoring to avoid Glycol bromide breakthrough.

core_unit_functions.antiscalant_and_prefiltration: protect membranes from fouling and scaling
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: reduce dissolved salts and reject radium and many co-contaminants
core_unit_functions.permeate_uv_advanced_oxidation: polish any low-level organic contaminant passage and support potable reuse confidence
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate corrosivity and mineral balance

applicability_rationale: RO is technically capable and offers broad removal for brackish groundwater, including salinity reduction and strong radionuclide rejection.
The route may be attractive if the project places additional value on desalting the groundwater before blending into potable reuse product water.
Permeate polishing with UV-AOP adds another barrier for trace organic control.
This is a strong technical option but not the best balance under the stated residuals, cost, and footprint trade-offs.
potential_risks: Brine management is a major drawback for inland brackish groundwater sites and may be more difficult than handling spent media or ion-exchange residuals.
High hardness and sulfate create scaling risk and demand careful pretreatment and membrane operation.
RO alone is not ideal as the sole justification when the main target organic is volatile and can be removed by simpler methods.
System recovery constraints can increase reject volume and total cost.

core_unit_functions.air_stripping_packed_tower: remove volatile Glycol bromide from water
core_unit_functions.lime_softening_clarification_for_radium: reduce radium by co-precipitation with hardness removal

applicability_rationale: Each target contaminant is addressed by a conventional and technically defensible unit process.
This route may appeal where chemical softening is already familiar to the utility or where hardness reduction is strongly desired.
It is included as a technically capable but lower-ranked option because the combined tower plus softening train does not align well with the compact-site objective.
potential_risks: The combined need for air-phase controls, clarifier footprint, sludge handling, and filtration makes this route operationally burdensome.
Radioactive sludge handling can be more challenging than resin regeneration waste in a compact reuse facility.
Civil and building requirements for both tower and clarification basins can be prohibitive on a constrained site.
Process integration complexity is high relative to the incremental treatment benefit.

preferred_rank_pattern: A hybrid route using adsorption or stripping for Glycol bromide plus ion exchange for radium should rank near the top
RO-based treatment should appear as technically strong but should not rank first because of concentrate, energy, and cost trade-offs
Clarification or lime-softening based routes should be considered plausible but downgraded under severe footprint limitations

must_reflect: strict finished-water target for a trace halogenated organic in a potable reuse context
space limitation that penalizes large clarifiers and tall or bulky process layouts
moderate cost tolerance but not enough to justify high-brine or highly energy-intensive treatment as the default choice
importance of minimizing difficult radioactive residuals and inland brine disposal burdens

must_contain: why Glycol bromide can be treated by volatilization and/or activated carbon rather than only by membranes
why radium commonly favors cation exchange or lime softening in groundwater
why high TDS and hardness make membrane and scaling considerations relevant
why compactness and residuals management change the ranking among technically feasible options

should_mention: breakthrough monitoring for Glycol bromide on GAC
off-gas treatment needs if air stripping is used
radium-bearing residuals or regeneration waste handling
RO concentrate disposal and scaling risk
